content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Consolidation with mitigation (closure as default, compensatory measures)'. Portugal's experience shows that school closure intensity from 2005–2014 did not automatically improve learning outcomes, and conditions of consolidation (travel burden, facility quality, community voice, pedagogical support) matter more for rural student access and equity than closure decisions alone.
opportunities: Enables investment concentration in fewer, better-equipped consolidated sites, following the Portuguese centros escolares pattern of matched receiving-site investment.
Statutory transport guarantees and targeted support provide a formal, legally enforceable floor of protection for displaced pupils, an improvement over ad hoc arrangements.
strengths: Portugal closed 4,197 primary schools between 2005/2006 and 2012/2013, initially targeting schools with fewer than 10 students (2005), then expanding to schools with fewer than 21 students (2010).

In Portugal's Central Region, 90% of all school closures (1,981 establishments) occurred in 2005–2014, with the process stabilizing after 2015; the Viseu Dão Lafões inter-municipal area alone lost 527 establishments (2005–2022).
threats: Compensatory measures (transport guarantees, TEIP-style support) require sustained multi-year funding; without a specified binding funding-commitment mechanism (e.g., ring-fenced budget line, multi-year statutory allocation), these measures risk being cut in future budget cycles once the initial closure has already occurred and political attention has moved on.

Closure decisions, even with consultation, may trigger community mobilization (as in the 2007 Erdőkürt 'Human Chain for Rural Schools' precedent), and limited judicial review (legality-only, not policy-merit) means affected communities have few formal channels to contest closure substance.
weaknesses: To what extent did Portugal's school closures directly cause village depopulation, versus depopulation driving closure demand? The causal direction is contested in existing sources (sociologist advocacy claims versus no quantitative longitudinal study).

What proportion of Hungarian depopulating villages have the demographic and geographic characteristics (sub-2,000 population, travel distance > 25 minutes to next school) that would align with Portuguese closure scenarios? No baseline census data on rural school catchment distance has been cited.
